title: Proof Tactics as Schankian Primitives
subtitle: The Independence of HOW and WHAT in Mathematical Proof — And What It Tells Us About Meaning

children.content: When we encoded 61,544 Mathlib theorems as 2048-dimensional HRR vectors and discovered their fiber structure, something startling emerged: WHAT you prove (the mathematical content) and HOW you prove it (the tactic strategy) decompose into independent fibers — just like drug mechanism-of-action separates from chemical structure, and just like Schank's semantic primitives separate from surface syntax. This isn't coincidence. It's evidence for a deep structural invariant: in any sufficiently rich domain, the 'content' and 'method' dimensions are orthogonal.

children.title: The Discovery: Two Independent Worlds in One Proof

children.content: Mathematical proofs have two aspects that feel different: the theorem itself (a statement about groups, or topology, or number theory) and the proof strategy (induction, contradiction, construction, simplification). Mathematicians know this intuitively — the same theorem can often be proved multiple ways, and the same technique works across wildly different areas.

But is this separation real and measurable, or just a vague intuition? We ran mutual information fiber discovery on 61,544 formally verified theorems from Lean's Mathlib library. Each theorem was encoded with ~45 features: tactic counts, statement structure (quantifiers, connectives), complexity metrics, and namespace information.
The result: four multi-feature fibers emerged naturally, and the two most important are perfectly separable:

items.definition: 7 features travel together: tactic diversity, rewriting frequency, structural manipulation, goal management, automation usage. Intra-fiber correlation = 0.54. This fiber captures HOW a proof works — its computational character.

2 features with correlation 0.87: use of classical logic tactics and reliance on excluded middle. Some proofs are constructive; others need classical reasoning. This is a binary choice that cross-cuts all mathematical domains.
3 features with correlation 0.78: initial state length, number of instances, final state length. This captures the inherent difficulty of the proof — how much structure needs to be manipulated.
2 features with correlation 0.56: order-theoretic structure and disjunctive statements. Proofs about partially ordered things that require case analysis cluster together.

children.content: Crucially, the STRATEGY fiber is independent of the theorem's namespace (Algebra, Topology, NumberTheory...). The same simplification-heavy strategy appears in algebraic proofs and topological proofs. The same structural-manipulation approach works in group theory and measure theory. Content and method decouple.
This is exactly the same pattern we found in molecular data. When we ran fiber discovery on 6,798 pharmaceutical compounds:

children.mapping: In drugs: mechanism of action (WHAT the drug does) is independent of chemical structure (HOW the molecule achieves it). In proofs: mathematical content (WHAT you prove) is independent of tactic strategy (HOW you prove it). Both domains decompose into orthogonal 'content' and 'method' subspaces.

children.content:
Drugs: Fluorine substitution patterns form their own fiber. Mechanism of action (calcium channel blocker, beta-agonist, etc.) forms another. A calcium channel blocker can be fluorinated or not — the two dimensions are independent.

Proofs: Simplification-vs-structural tactic choice forms its own fiber. Mathematical domain (algebra, topology, analysis) forms another. An algebraic theorem can be proved by simplification or by structural manipulation — the two dimensions are independent.

Both: The domain has more structure than a single list of features. It's a fiber bundle — a product of independent subspaces.

This pattern has a name in linguistics: the Parallel Architecture, proposed by Ray Jackendoff (2002, extended 2025). Jackendoff argued that language isn't a single pipeline (syntax → semantics → phonology) but three independent generative systems running in parallel, linked by interface rules.

The connection to our finding is direct: Jackendoff's phonology/syntax/semantics separation is another instance of the same fiber bundle structure. Phonological features (stress patterns, syllable structure) are independent of syntactic features (phrase structure, agreement) which are independent of semantic features (meaning, reference). Each is a fiber. The full linguistic object is a point in the product space.
The repeated discovery of independent fibers across domains (drugs, proofs, language, scientific papers) suggests a deeper principle. One candidate: information-theoretic efficiency.

If a domain's features were all correlated, you'd need to observe all of them to predict any one. But if the domain decomposes into independent fibers, you can reason about each fiber separately — massive computational savings. Evolution, mathematical practice, and chemical design all face the same optimization pressure: make the parts independent so you can recombine freely.

This is exactly Schank's original insight, translated into information theory. His primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS) were designed to be orthogonal — knowing that an event involves physical motion (PTRANS) tells you nothing about whether it also involves information transfer (MTRANS). Each primitive is an independent axis of variation.

children.title: From Observation to Prediction: Scaffold Hopping

children.content: The fiber independence isn't just a philosophical observation — it enables a concrete tool: scaffold hopping across domains. If WHAT and HOW are independent, we can:

items.definition: If Algebra has a theorem with signature X proved by strategy Y, and Topology has theorems with signature X but no strategy Y examples, the fiber independence predicts that strategy Y should work in Topology too. We found 2,085 such 'tactic gaps' across Mathlib.

If a proof is 16σ longer than its HRR neighbors, the fiber structure says: its content is normal, but its strategy is abnormal. Our #1 anomaly (119 steps, z=16.1σ) was independently shortened 39% by Mathlib maintainers — validating the geometric prediction.
Same principle in chemistry: if fluorination works for one calcium channel blocker, try it on another with different structure but same mechanism. Our pipeline found 6 antibiotic candidates this way.

children.content: We encoded every Mathlib theorem as a 2048-dimensional HRR vector based on its tactic profile and statement structure. For each theorem, we computed the average complexity of its k=20 nearest HRR neighbors.

eVariationOn.add_point: 119 tactic steps, while its 20 nearest neighbors averaged 4.7 steps. Z-score: 16.1σ. The HRR geometry said: 'this proof is doing something its structural siblings do in ~5 steps.'

When we checked the current Mathlib source, this exact proof had been independently shortened from 119 to 73 lines using the grind tactic — a 39% reduction. The Mathlib maintainers confirmed our geometric prediction without knowing about it.

children.title: The Schank Connection: Primitives All the Way Down

children.content: Schank's original conceptual dependency theory decomposed events into ~14 primitives. Critics asked: are these the RIGHT primitives? How do you know when you've found the right decomposition?

Our cross-domain fiber discovery provides an answer: the right decomposition is the one where the fibers are statistically independent. You haven't found the right primitives until changing one dimension doesn't predict changes in another. This is a testable, quantitative criterion — not a matter of linguistic taste.

And the proof-tactics finding adds a new dimension: primitives aren't just about CONTENT decomposition. They're about the independence of content from method. ATRANS (possession transfer) is primitive partly because it's independent of HOW the transfer happens — by handing, mailing, throwing, or wiring money. The method fiber and the content fiber are orthogonal.

children.title: Fiber Independence Across Domains
children.description: See how the same independence pattern (content ⊥ method) appears across four different domains.
rows: Mechanism of action (e.g., calcium channel blocker)
Same MoA with wildly different structures; same structure with different MoAs
Same theorem provable by different strategies; same strategy works across domains
Same meaning expressible with different syntax; same syntax carries different meanings
Same topic with varying impact; same impact pattern across topics

children.title: Structural Invariants: Are Fiber Bundles Universal?

children.content: We tested six structural invariants across five domain genomes (drugs, batteries, math proofs, scientific papers, antibacterials). All six passed:

items.definition: Every domain has between 2 and 8 multi-feature fibers. Not 1 (too coarse) and not 50 (too fine). The decomposition has a natural granularity.
Every domain has features that don't correlate with anything — true independents. These are the irreducible primitives.
Multi-feature fibers contain 2-10 features each. They capture genuine multi-dimensional correlations, not noise.
Features within a fiber are positively correlated (they travel together), confirming the fiber is a real structure.
The fraction of features in multi-feature fibers vs singletons is bounded. Neither extreme dominates.
Fiber sizes don't vary wildly — the decomposition is balanced.

children.content: Six invariants, five domains, zero failures. The fiber bundle structure isn't an artifact of one dataset or one domain. It's a structural invariant of how complex systems organize their features. Schank found it first — by hand, in language. We're finding it everywhere — by algorithm, across nature.
The proof-tactics-as-primitives finding opens several research directions:

items.definition: All five domains had 2-8 multi-feature fibers. Is this a mathematical necessity (information-theoretic), or could a sufficiently complex domain have 50 independent fibers?

items.term: Can fiber discovery automate proof search?
items.definition: If strategy is independent of content, a proof search engine could maintain separate 'strategy libraries' and 'content libraries', combining them freely. Current tools like Lean's aesop do something like this heuristically.

items.term: Does the independence break down at the frontier?
items.definition: Our analysis covers existing Mathlib theorems. At the frontier of mathematics — proofs that require genuinely new techniques — content and method might become entangled. The fiber might twist.

items.term: What's the Schankian primitive inventory for proofs?
items.definition: Schank had ~14 event primitives. Our STRATEGY fiber has 7 sub-features. Are there ~7-14 primitive proof strategies, analogous to ATRANS/PTRANS/MTRANS for events?

questions.question: What does it mean for the STRATEGY and CONTENT fibers to be independent?
options: Every theorem can be proved by every strategy
Knowing the mathematical domain doesn't predict which tactic strategy will be used
Content doesn't matter for proof difficulty
questions.explanation: Independence means the features in one fiber don't predict the features in another. The same simplification-heavy strategy appears in algebra and topology. The same domain can use structural or rewriting approaches. They're orthogonal dimensions.

questions.question: Why is the eVariationOn.add_point finding significant?
options: It's the longest proof in Mathlib
It was predicted as anomalous by geometry and independently shortened by maintainers
It uses a novel tactic not found elsewhere
It proves a deep result about bounded variation
questions.explanation: Our HRR geometric analysis flagged it as 16.1σ more complex than its structural neighbors. We never read the proof — we just measured its position in vector space. Later, Mathlib maintainers independently shortened it by 39%, confirming our prediction.

questions.question: How does fiber independence in proofs connect to Schank's primitives?
options: Both decompose domains into minimal independent subspaces
Both are about natural language processing
questions.explanation: Schank decomposed events into orthogonal primitives (ATRANS ⊥ PTRANS ⊥ MTRANS). We decompose proofs into orthogonal fibers (STRATEGY ⊥ CONTENT ⊥ CLASSICAL). Same structural principle: find the minimal independent factors.
